plainMeaning: Victory to the teaching of the Jinas — the most auspicious of all auspicious things, the cause of every good, and the foremost of all paths.
recitation: sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ, sarva-kalyāṇa-kāraṇaṁ; pradhānaṁ sarva-dharmāṇāṁ, jainaṁ jayati śāsanam.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sarva ('whole, entire'); here the first member of the compound sarva-maṅgala.
wordByWord.notes: First of three occurrences of sarva in the verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit maṅgala ('that which is auspicious / brings welfare'); same maṅgala as in the verse-44 sense of benediction.
wordByWord.notes: Joined with the next word: sarva-maṅgala = 'all auspicious things'.

wordByWord.gloss: the auspiciousness (of), the very essence of blessing
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit māṅgalya, abstract/vṛddhi derivative of maṅgala ('auspiciousness itself'). Acc./nom. sg. neut.
wordByWord.notes: sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ = 'the auspiciousness of all auspicious things' — the blessing that makes other blessings auspicious.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sarva; second occurrence, opening the compound sarva-kalyāṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Parallels the first pāda's sarva for rhetorical balance.

wordByWord.gloss: the cause of all welfare / well-being
wordByWord.etymology: kalyāṇa ('welfare, good, the auspicious good') + kāraṇa (√kṛ, 'cause, instrument'); nom. sg. neut. In Jain usage kalyāṇa also evokes the five kalyāṇaka life-events of a Jina.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as one token in the book; semantically sarva-kalyāṇa-kāraṇaṁ, 'the cause of every good'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pra-dhāna (pra + √dhā, 'set foremost'); nom. sg. neut. agreeing with śāsanam.
wordByWord.notes: Governs the genitive sarva-dharmāṇāṁ that follows.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sarva; third occurrence, qualifying dharmāṇāṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Third anaphoric sarva — the triple repetition is deliberate stylistic emphasis.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit dharma (√dhṛ, 'that which upholds'); gen. pl. masc. Here 'paths of righteousness / religious orders'.
wordByWord.notes: pradhānaṁ sarva-dharmāṇāṁ = 'the foremost of all dharmas'.

wordByWord.gloss: of/relating to the Jina; the Jain (teaching)
wordByWord.etymology: vṛddhi derivative of Jina ('spiritual victor'); jaina = 'pertaining to the Jina'. Nom. sg. neut. qualifying śāsanam.
wordByWord.notes: The adjective and its noun frame the verb: jainaṁ … śāsanam, 'the Jina's teaching'.

wordByWord.etymology: √ji ('to conquer, win'), 3rd sg. present active. Cognate with the very name Jina.
wordByWord.notes: Living present tense — an ongoing, eternal victory, not a single past conquest.

wordByWord.gloss: teaching, doctrine, the ordered community of the Jina
wordByWord.etymology: √śās ('to instruct, govern'); śāsana = 'instruction, rule, the dispensation'. Nom. sg. neut., the grammatical subject.
wordByWord.notes: In Jain idiom the jina-śāsana is both the doctrine and the fourfold saṅgha that carries it.

literalTranslation: The auspiciousness of all auspicious things, the cause of all welfare, foremost among all dharmas — the teaching of the Jina is victorious.
idiomaticTranslation: Most blessed of all that is blessed, the wellspring of every good, supreme among all paths of righteousness — the śāsana of the Jinas reigns triumphant.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the previous stanza urged the seeker who longs for the supreme state, or for wide-spread renown, to revere the world-uplifting word of the Jina (jiṇa-vayaṇa), this verse delivers the natural climax: it does not merely praise the teaching — it crowns it. Three sweeping superlatives mount in a rising tricolon, each opening with sarva ('all'): the śāsana is the auspiciousness within all auspiciousness, the cause behind all welfare, the foremost of all dharmas. The repeated sarva is not loose hyperbole but a deliberate logical move — whatever blessing, good, or right path exists anywhere derives its worth from this one source. The fourth pāda then resolves the build-up into a single ringing verb: jayati, 'is victorious.' Note the present tense: the conquest is perpetual and present, echoing the very name Jina ('victor') from which jainaṁ is formed — so that the teaching of the Victors is itself ever-victorious. The verse is grammatically tight: three neuter nominatives (māṅgalyaṁ, kāraṇaṁ, pradhānaṁ) all predicate the single subject śāsanam, with jainaṁ as its adjective. Placed as the first seal of benediction after the hymn proper, it converts devotion into proclamation — the worshipper who has just sought refuge now declares aloud the supremacy of that refuge.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The 'victory' here carries no note of sectarian conquest over others; in Jain thought jaya is the inner triumph over the passions (kaṣāya) that the Jinas embodied and that their śāsana transmits. To call the teaching sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ aligns it with the Navkar's culika, which names the fivefold homage 'the first and foremost of all auspicious things' — the same supreme-maṅgala logic applied here to the whole dispensation. kalyāṇa further resonates with the five kalyāṇakas (the auspicious life-moments of every Tīrthaṅkara), grounding 'cause of all welfare' in the salvific biography of the Jinas. śāsana denotes both the doctrine and the fourfold saṅgha (sādhu, sādhvī, śrāvaka, śrāvikā) that bears it — so the verse exalts the living tradition, not an abstraction.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as the protective smaraṇa closes, this stanza lets the practitioner convert private petition into confident affirmation: having asked for fearless peace, one now rests in the certainty that the path itself is supremely auspicious. Many Jains use it as an everyday maṅgala to open studies, ceremonies, and undertakings — a one-breath act of taking refuge.

crossReferences.text: Navakāra cūlikā: 'maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ, paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ' (foremost of all auspicious things)
The five kalyāṇaka events of a Tīrthaṅkara (cyavana, janma, dīkṣā, kevala-jñāna, nirvāṇa)

sources.title: Shasana, Śāsana — 29 definitions (Sanskrit/Prakrit/Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss and √śās etymology of śāsana as teaching/rule/dispensation, including its Jain sense.

sources.title: Mangalya, Māṅgalya — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Confirming māṅgalya as the abstract/vṛddhi derivative of maṅgala ('auspiciousness itself').

sources.title: Kalyana, Kalyāṇa — 38 definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of kalyāṇa as welfare/the auspicious good and its Jain kalyāṇaka resonance.

sources.title: Jain stotra anthologies and maṅgala-stuti collections (jainqq digital library)
sources.consultedFor: Identification of this stanza as a standard Jain maṅgala verse appended to recitations; sense of jaina-śāsana.

notes: Pure Sanskrit maṅgala stanza (anuṣṭubh). Orthography preserved verbatim: the book separates 'મંગલ માંગલ્યં' but joins 'કલ્યાણકારણં' into one token, and writes final -m as anusvāra (જૈનં, શાસનં, માંગલ્યં, કારણં, પ્રધાનં). The phrasing closely parallels the well-known Hindu benediction 'sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalye…' (Durgā-saptaśatī / Devī-māhātmya); the Jain recension redirects the auspiciousness to the jaina-śāsana itself rather than to a deity — a literary parallel, not a borrowing-claim. This stanza circulates widely across Jain traditions as an independent maṅgala, so its specific authorship within this hymn is not asserted. 'jayati' is firmly present indicative; no variant reading affecting sense was found.
